docs.title: 3.5 hours, 9 labs — build an NSO service that survives real drift
docs.text:
For network engineers and automation leads working through a guided Cisco NSO hands-on lab with XRd peers.

Time ~3 h 30 min total·Labs 9 hands-on·NSO 6.5 (sandbox)

docs.title: Reference topology
docs.text: Lab sandbox — workstation, NSO, and XRd peers

docs.title: Lab journey
docs.text:
Hands-on lab journeyLabTitleTimeDescription1Connect to the Workstation10 minAccess the lab VM and terminal 2Install NSO and NEDs35 minLocal NSO install, environment, IOS-XR NED 3Register XRd Routers20 minDevice definitions and sync-from 4Configure Devices20 minPush changes via NSO Web UI 5Rollbacks20 minRollback files and revert commits 6Out-of-Band Sync20 minDetect and reconcile OOB changes 7Device Groups & Templates25 minGroups and templates at scale 8Create a Service30 minintentional break Python-and-template service package 9RBAC Access Control30 minUsers, authgroups, NACM rules

Before you begin

Provision your sandbox and open a console to the linux-host workstation. Commands assume that environment unless noted.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Connect to the Ubuntu lab desktop through the hosted Remote Access console.
  • Open Visual Studio Code and use the integrated terminal on linux-host.
  • Verify workstation identity using basic shell commands.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Console & desktopVS Code & terminalVerify shell
  • Console & desktop — 3 min
  • VS Code & terminal — 5 min
  • Verify shell — 2 min

Total — 10 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• You can sign in to the hosted lab environment and open Remote Access (or equivalent) for this workbook.
  • You have a modern browser; pop-up blockers are disabled or allow-listed for the lab portal if your site requires it.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text: You will use the linux-host Ubuntu desktop and its integrated terminal for all following labs. Choose Ubuntu 24.04 Desktop unless your facilitator directs otherwise.

docs.title: Step 1: Open the lab VM console
docs.text:
1. From the lab environment, select Ubuntu 24.04 Desktop (recommended) or Win11 if you must use Windows for this session.
  2. Open VM Console from the Remote Access menu (wording may vary slightly by platform).
  1. On Windows, if you see “You’re almost done setting up your PC,” choose Remind me in 3 days to reach the desktop.

Wait until you see the desktop background and can move the pointer inside the VM.

docs.title: Step 2: Orient yourself on the desktop
docs.text: Alternatively, if you chose Ubuntu 24.04 Desktop, you will see a desktop similar to this:

docs.title: Step 3: Open Visual Studio Code and the integrated terminal
docs.text:
1. Open Visual Studio Code from the applications menu or the dock.
  2. Open the integrated terminal (Terminal → New Terminal or the shortcut shown in your environment).
  1. Stay in this terminal for command examples in later labs.

docs.title: Step 4: Confirm the workstation hostname
docs.text:
Run:

hostname

Expected output:

linux-host

If the name differs, ask your facilitator—some environments use a different label, but the workflow is the same.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Confirm you are on linux-host and the shell is responsive. Run these in order in the same terminal:

hostname
whoami
pwd

Expected output:

linux-host
cisco
/home/cisco

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Black or frozen VM console after Remote Access.

Cause Browser session timed out, GPU acceleration glitch, or console tab lost focus.

Fix Refresh the Remote Access page, close other heavy tabs, and reopen VM Console. If it persists, snapshot-restore per Reset the Lab.

Symptom: Integrated terminal in VS Code does not open or closes immediately.

Cause VS Code started before the desktop session finished loading, or extension host crashed.

Fix Quit VS Code fully and reopen from the menu. If needed, use an external terminal app temporarily and continue Lab 2 from there.

If the desktop or console is unusable after these steps, restore the lab VM snapshot and retry. For the full reset playbook, see Reset the Lab.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Locate and extract the NSO free-trial installer for the release pinned in this workbook ({{ nso_version }}).
  • Perform a local NSO install, create an instance, and confirm the daemon is running.
  • Install the Cisco IOS-XR NED package and reload packages in the Web UI.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Extract installerInstall & instanceWeb UI & NED
  • Extract installer — 5 min
  • Install & instance — 22 min
  • Web UI & NED — 8 min

Total — 35 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Lab 1: Connect to the Workstation completed — you have a shell on linux-host.
  • The NSO free-trial bundle for this workbook is available under your home directory (folder name may match NSO-{{ nso_version }}-free or your site’s layout — adjust paths below if your facilitator uses a different bundle).

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text: Commands use {{ nso_version }} so they track mkdocs.yml extra.nso_version. If your lab still ships a different directory name, substitute your path inside the command fences only.

docs.title: Step 1: Locate the installer files
docs.text:
Open VS Code and work in the Terminal panel (or any shell on linux-host).

Navigate to the NSO installer directory and list the available binaries:

cd ~/NSO-{{ nso_version }}-free/
ls *.bin

Expected output:

ncs-{{ nso_version }}-cisco-asa-6.x-freetrial.signed.bin
ncs-{{ nso_version }}-cisco-ios-6.x-freetrial.signed.bin
ncs-{{ nso_version }}-cisco-iosxr-7.x-freetrial.signed.bin
ncs-{{ nso_version }}-cisco-nx-5.x-freetrial.signed.bin
nso-{{ nso_version }}-freetrial.container-image-build.linux.x86_64.signed.bin
nso-{{ nso_version }}-freetrial.container-image-prod.linux.x86_64.signed.bin
nso-{{ nso_version }}-freetrial.linux.x86_64.signed.bin

(Exact NED patch versions may differ; you need the Linux nso-…freetrial.linux.x86_64.signed.bin and the IOS-XR ncs-…-cisco-iosxr-….signed.bin.)

docs.title: Step 2: Extract the NSO installer
docs.text:
Create a work directory and extract the signed installer (output is verbose; this can take a few minutes):

mkdir -p work
cd work
bash ../nso-{{ nso_version }}-freetrial.linux.x86_64.signed.bin --skip-verification

docs.title: Step 3: Install NSO locally
docs.text:
Run the extracted installer with the --local-install flag:

bash nso-{{ nso_version }}.linux.x86_64.installer.bin --local-install ~/NSO-INSTALL

Expected output:

INFO  Using temporary directory /tmp/ncs_installer.XXXXXX to stage NCS installation bundle
INFO  Unpacked ncs-{{ nso_version }} in /home/cisco/NSO-INSTALL
INFO  Found and unpacked corresponding DOCUMENTATION_PACKAGE
INFO  Found and unpacked corresponding EXAMPLE_PACKAGE
INFO  Found and unpacked corresponding JAVA_PACKAGE
INFO  Generating default SSH hostkey (this may take some time)
INFO  NCS installation complete

(Additional INFO lines about SSH keys, certificates, NETSIM, and ncsrc are normal.)

docs.title: Step 4: Source the NSO environment
docs.text:
cd ~/NSO-INSTALL/
source ncsrc

docs.title: Step 5: Create an NSO instance
docs.text:
ncs-setup --dest nso-instance
cd nso-instance/
ls

Expected output:

README.ncs  logs  ncs-cdb  ncs.conf  packages  scripts  state

docs.title: Step 6: Configure WebUI access
docs.text:
Starting with NSO {{ nso_version }}, WebUI and RESTCONF may only allow access via the server hostname. To allow access by IP address, edit ncs.conf in your instance (for example ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/ncs.conf — confirm with your facilitator).

  1. Open ncs.conf and find the <webui> … </webui> block (create it under the top-level config if your template does not include it).
  2. Inside <webui>, add a <server-alias> whose value is the same IPv4 address learners will type in the browser (the management address of linux-host running NSO — not the router address from Lab 4).

Example (RFC 5737 documentation range — substitute your lab IP):

<server-alias>198.51.100.27</server-alias>
  1. Ensure the Web UI listens where you expect (often <transport><tcp><ip>0.0.0.0</ip> … under <webui>). If your file already has TCP/WebUI transport settings, do not duplicate them — only add or fix server-alias so it matches the URL you publish in Step 9.

Consistency check: server-alias = IP in http://<IP>:8080/... (same host as NSO). It must not be the XRd device management IP (Lab 4 uses a different example address for xr-1).

Security Note

Binding Web UI to 0.0.0.0 is for lab use only. In production, restrict access to specific interfaces.

docs.title: Step 7: Start NSO
docs.text:
ncs

(No stdout is normal; the daemon starts in the background. Use the next step to confirm.)

docs.title: Step 8: Confirm NSO is running
docs.text:
ncs --status | grep started

Expected output:

status: started

docs.title: Step 9: Access the Web UI
docs.text:
Open Firefox and navigate to the login URL your environment provides. Example:

http://198.51.100.27:8080/login.html

Default Credentials

Username: admin | Password: admin

After logging in, you will see the NSO main page showing Devices, Services, Config editor, and Tools tiles.

Navigate to the Config Editor section.

docs.title: Extract the NED package
docs.text:
cd ~/NSO-{{ nso_version }}-free/work/
bash ../ncs-{{ nso_version }}-cisco-iosxr-7.x-freetrial.signed.bin --skip-verification

(Replace 7.x in the filename with the exact IOS-XR NED package name from ls *.bin in Step 1 if it differs.)

docs.title: Copy the NED archive into the instance
docs.text:
cp ncs-{{ nso_version }}-cisco-iosxr-*.tar.gz ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/packages/

Expected output:

(No output on success; verify with ls ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/packages/ if needed.)

docs.title: Reload packages in the Web UI
docs.text:
1. Go to the NSO Web UI.
  2. Navigate to ncs:packages.
  3. Select Actions → Reload → Run reload action.

After reloading, you should see the cisco-iosxr-cli package in the list.

Verify

Open Devices — it should be empty until Lab 3.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Confirm the daemon and package reload:

ncs --status | grep started

Expected output:

status: started

In the Web UI, confirm ncs:packages lists cisco-ios-xr (name may vary slightly by build).

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Installer or ncs-setup fails with permission or existing directory errors.

Cause Prior partial install or wrong working directory leaves files in ~/NSO-INSTALL or work/.

Fix Stop NSO (`ncs --stop`), remove the partial trees after backup if needed, or restore the VM snapshot — see Rollback below and Reset the Lab.

Symptom: Web UI returns connection refused or TLS errors after editing ncs.conf.

Cause Wrong server-alias IP, daemon not restarted, or firewall blocking the port.

Fix Fix `server-alias` to match how learners browse (IP vs hostname), restart `ncs`, and retest. Ask the class to use the exact URL you published.

Rollback

If you must discard this install and start over on the same VM (destructive):

ncs --stop 2>/dev/null || true
rm -rf ~/NSO-INSTALL ~/NSO-{{ nso_version }}-free/work/nso-instance

Prefer Reset the Lab (snapshot restore) when the system is in an unknown state.

If NSO will not start or packages will not reload after multiple attempts, restore the VM snapshot and repeat this lab. Details: Reset the Lab.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Verify XRd Docker containers are running on the lab host.
  • Load device definitions into NSO and confirm devices appear in the Web UI.
  • Run sync-from to populate the NSO CDB with device configuration.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Verify containersLoad devices & sync
  • Verify containers — 8 min
  • Load devices & sync — 12 min

Total — 20 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Lab 2: Install NSO and NEDs completed — NSO daemon is started and the cisco-ios-xr package shows in ncs:packages.
  • You can open the NSO Web UI and run commands in a terminal on linux-host.

docs.title: Step 1: Verify XRd containers are running
docs.text:
Two XRd routers run as Docker containers on the same host. Confirm they are up:

docker ps

Expected output:

CONTAINER ID   IMAGE                             COMMAND                CREATED      STATUS      NAMES
…              ios-xr/xrd-control-plane:…        "/usr/local/sbin/init" …         Up …        xr-2
…              ios-xr/xrd-control-plane:…        "/usr/local/sbin/init" …         Up …        xr-1

You should see xr-1 and xr-2 with the same ios-xr/xrd-control-plane image (tag may differ from the example). Other lab containers (for example source / dest) may appear — that is expected.

docs.title: Step 2: Load device definitions
docs.text:
A prepared XML template defines both routers. From the bundle directory (adjust if your site places devices.xml elsewhere):

cd ~/NSO-{{ nso_version }}-free/
ncs_load -l -m devices.xml

Expected output:

(Empty stdout is normal when load succeeds.)

Flag Meaning -l Load operation -m Merge with existing configuration

docs.title: Step 3: Verify devices in the Web UI
docs.text:
1. Open the NSO Web UI.
  2. Click Devices.
  3. Confirm xr-1 and xr-2 are listed.

docs.title: Step 4: Sync device configuration
docs.text:
The first action after adding devices is sync-from so the NSO CDB matches each device’s running configuration.

  1. Select all devices (or each device in turn).
  2. Execute sync-from.

If sync-from fails

Run Fetch SSH Host Keys first, then retry sync-from.

After a successful sync-from, NSO’s CDB holds the full running configuration of both routers.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Confirm both devices appear in NSO (source the environment as in Lab 2, then run one non-interactive CLI session):

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show devices list" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

Expected output:

NAME  ADDRESS     DESCRIPTION  ADMIN-STATE  NODE  TAG
xr-1  …                        unlocked     …
xr-2  …                        unlocked     …

(Addresses and columns follow your lab’s devices.xml.)

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: docker ps does not list xr-1 and xr-2.

Cause Containers stopped, Docker not running, or wrong host session.

Fix On linux-host: `docker ps -a` and start containers per your lab guide; if broken, use Reset the Lab.

Symptom: sync-from fails with authentication or host key errors.

Cause SSH keys not fetched or management IP mismatch in NSO vs device.

Fix Run Fetch SSH Host Keys, verify addresses in `devices.xml`, retry sync-from. See Reset the Lab if CDB is corrupt.

If devices cannot be added or sync-from never succeeds after fetching SSH host keys, see Reset the Lab for snapshot restore versus re-loading device definitions.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Navigate the NSO Web UI to a device configuration path on xr-1.
  • Stage a candidate configuration change and commit it through Commit Manager.
  • Verify the change on the device CLI.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Web UI edit & commitDevice CLI verify
  • Web UI edit & commit — 10 min
  • Device CLI verify — 10 min

Total — 20 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Lab 3: Register XRd Routers completed — xr-1 and xr-2 show as unlocked and sync-from has succeeded at least once.
  • You can open the NSO Web UI and SSH from linux-host to the device management address (your lab sheet lists the address).

docs.title: Step 1: Browse device configuration in the Web UI
docs.text:
Navigate to xr-1 in the Web UI to inspect interface configuration. Use the search bar or expand the configuration tree.

Follow this path to reach the GigabitEthernet IPv4 address container:

/ncs:devices/device{xr-1}/config/cisco-ios-xr:interface/GigabitEthernet{0/0/0/0}/ipv4/address/

docs.title: Step 2: Make a configuration change
docs.text:
1. Click Edit Config.
  2. Change the IPv4 address from 10.1.1.3 to 10.1.1.30 (documentation-style lab addressing).

Candidate Configuration

A green bar appears on edited fields — this is candidate configuration. Changes apply to the device only after you commit.

docs.title: Step 3: Review and commit
docs.text:
1. Open Tools → Commit Manager.
  2. Open the config tab for a diff view.
  1. Review the change, then click Commit.

After commit, NSO creates a rollback file automatically (covered further in Lab 5).

docs.title: Step 4: Verify on the device
docs.text:
SSH to xr-1 and confirm the interface address in running configuration.

ssh admin@198.51.100.2

(Use the management IP your lab assigns; 198.51.100.2 is an example documentation address.)

At the XR prompt, run:

show run interface gigabitEthernet 0/0/0/0

Expected output:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
 ipv4 address 10.1.1.30 255.255.255.0
!

(Timestamps and banner lines may appear above the interface block.)

Password

If prompted: cisco123 (or the password your facilitator provides).

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
From linux-host, confirm the committed value is visible in NSO (same shell environment as prior labs):

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show configuration devices device xr-1 config cisco-ios-xr:interface GigabitEthernet 0/0/0/0 ipv4 address" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

Expected output:

address 10.1.1.30 255.255.255.0

(Path or spacing may wrap differently; look for 10.1.1.30.)

If you already confirmed on the device CLI in Step 4, that counts as a second, independent check.

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Commit fails or device shows out-of-sync after commit.

Cause Another session holds a lock, or sync-from needed before edit.

Fix Close other NSO sessions, run check-sync, retry commit; see Lab 5 for rollback files.

Symptom: SSH to xr-1 refused or wrong interface output.

Cause Wrong management IP, routing from linux-host, or change not committed.

Fix Confirm IP in Web UI devices detail, ping from linux-host, re-check Commit Manager succeeded.

If commits leave the device and CDB out of sync, use Commit Manager rollbacks (see 5. Rollbacks) or Reset the Lab for a broader recovery path.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Locate rollback metadata in Commit Manager and load a rollback file.
  • Commit a loaded rollback as a new candidate and complete the workflow in Commit Manager.
  • Verify on the device CLI that configuration matches the rolled-back state.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Load rollbackVerify on device
  • Load rollback — 8 min
  • Verify on device — 12 min

Total — 20 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Lab 4: Configure Devices completed — at least one successful commit exists so NSO generated rollback files.
  • You can open the NSO Web UI as admin and SSH from linux-host to xr-1 (use the management IP from your lab sheet; examples use 198.51.100.2).

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text: NSO creates a rollback file for every commit so you can revert configuration changes safely.

docs.title: Step 1: Open Commit Manager
docs.text:
1. Go to Commit Manager.
  2. Click Load/Save.
  3. Select the rollback file that corresponds to the state before the Lab 4 address change (metadata shows User, Northbound interface, timestamp — pick the rollback that restores 10.1.1.3 on the interface).

docs.title: Step 2: Load the rollback
docs.text:
1. Click Load — the revert appears as a candidate configuration.
  2. Review the diff: the rollback reverses Lab 4's change (10.1.1.30 removed → 10.1.1.3 restored).
  1. Click Commit to apply.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify on the device
docs.text:
SSH to xr-1 and confirm the IPv4 address on the data interface is restored to 10.1.1.3.

ssh admin@198.51.100.2

(Replace with your lab management address.)

At the XR prompt:

show run interface gigabitEthernet 0/0/0/0

Expected output:

interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
 ipv4 address 10.1.1.3 255.255.255.0
!

(Timestamps and comments may vary.)

Key takeaway

Every NSO commit is reversible. Rollback files are your safety net when testing configuration changes.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Confirm the same address from NSO (optional second check):

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show configuration devices device xr-1 config cisco-ios-xr:interface GigabitEthernet 0/0/0/0 ipv4 address" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

Expected output:

address 10.1.1.3 255.255.255.0

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Load is greyed out or rollback list is empty.

Cause No prior commit in this session, or wrong NSO instance.

Fix Confirm you committed at least one change in Lab 4; check **Commit Manager** history and lab instructions for the correct instance path.

Symptom: Device still shows 10.1.1.30 after commit.

Cause Wrong rollback selected, or commit not applied to devices.

Fix Re-open the diff in Commit Manager, pick the rollback whose metadata matches the pre-change state, commit again, then re-run Check-Sync / sync-to if your site requires it.

If you cannot find a suitable rollback file or loads fail repeatedly, see Reset the Lab for VM-level reset options.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Create a configuration change directly on xr-1 (out-of-band) without using NSO.
  • Run Check-Sync in the Web UI to detect configuration drift.
  • Use Sync-To to align the device with NSO and verify the loopback is removed.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Out-of-band changeDetect & reconcile
  • Out-of-band change — 10 min
  • Detect & reconcile — 10 min

Total — 20 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Lab 5: Rollbacks completed — Check-Sync should be green for xr-1 before you start (restore with Reset the Lab if not).
  • You can SSH from linux-host to xr-1 (examples use 198.51.100.2 — use your lab address).

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text: One of NSO’s core workflows is detecting changes made on the device that did not go through NSO — out-of-band configuration.

docs.title: Step 1: Create an out-of-band change on xr-1
docs.text:
SSH to xr-1 and configure Loopback100 on the device CLI (not through NSO).

ssh admin@198.51.100.2

At the XR prompt, use a configuration session like this (prompts and timestamps will match your session):

RP/0/RP0/CPU0:xr-1#configure terminal
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:xr-1(config)#interface Loopback 100
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:xr-1(config-if)#description out-of-band change
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:xr-1(config-if)#commit
RP/0/RP0/CPU0:xr-1(config-if)#end

docs.title: Step 2: Verify the loopback on the device
docs.text:
show run interface Loopback 100

Expected output:

interface Loopback100
 description out-of-band change
!

docs.title: Step 3: Detect drift in NSO
docs.text:
In the NSO Web UI, run Check-Sync on xr-1.

Out-of-sync alert

NSO typically shows a red indicator because the device differs from the CDB. Open Compare-config to see the out-of-band change.

docs.title: Step 4: Resolve with Sync-To vs Sync-From
docs.text:
Option Action Result Sync-From Pull device config into NSO NSO accepts the out-of-band change Sync-To Push NSO’s config to the device Device matches NSO (drops OOB change)

To remove the loopback and match the prior NSO-known state, use Sync-To.

docs.title: Step 5: Execute Sync-To
docs.text:
1. Select xr-1 in the Web UI.
  2. Open the actions menu and choose Sync to.

docs.title: Step 6: Verify reconciliation
docs.text:
Run Check-Sync again — both devices should show in-sync.

On the device:

show run interface Loopback 100

Expected output:

% No such configuration item(s)

The Loopback 100 configuration is gone — NSO restored the device to its known-good state.

Key takeaway

Use check-sync regularly; pair it with sync-from or sync-to deliberately — sync-from ingests reality, sync-to enforces the NSO-intended configuration.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Confirm xr-1 is clear of Loopback 100 and NSO agrees:

  1. In the Web UI, Check-Sync for xr-1 is green.
  2. On the device CLI:
show run interface Loopback 100

Expected output:

% No such configuration item(s)

Optional — inspect sync from the NSO CLI (output varies by release; rely on the Web UI if this is empty):

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show devices check-sync" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Check-Sync stays red after Sync-To or shows a looping diff.

Cause Concurrent edits, partial sync, or SSH session left in config mode on the device.

Fix Close extra sessions, run **Check-Sync** again, then **Sync-To** once; capture Compare-config for the instructor. Use Reset the Lab if stuck.

Symptom: Loopback still present after Sync-To.

Cause Wrong device selected, or sync targeted a different NSO instance.

Fix Confirm **xr-1** address and authgroup; re-run **Sync-To** and verify with `show run interface Loopback 100` on the device.

If sync-to / sync-from loops or check-sync stays red with no clear diff, capture screenshots for your instructor and consider Reset the Lab if time is short.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Create a device group that contains xr-1 and xr-2.
  • Define a device template with IOS-XR DNS settings.
  • Run Apply-Template for the group and verify DNS configuration on a device.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Device groupTemplate & apply
  • Device group — 10 min
  • Template & apply — 15 min

Total — 25 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Lab 6: Out-of-Band Sync completed — both devices are in-sync and reachable.
  • You can open Configuration Editor and Commit Manager as admin.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text: Device groups and templates let you push configuration to multiple devices with one action.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a device group
docs.text:
1. Go to Devices → Device groups.
  2. Click + Add device group.
  3. Name it ios-xr-devices and click Create.
  1. Select xr-1 and xr-2, then + Add to device group.
  1. Click Create device group.

Both devices should appear in ios-xr-devices.

docs.title: Step 2: Create a device template
docs.text:
1. Open Configuration Editor.
  2. Select the ncs:devices module and click Edit config.
  1. In the template tile, click +.
  1. Choose NED cisco-iosxr, then Confirm.
  1. Open the NED subtree to edit template content.

docs.title: Step 3: Configure DNS in the template
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Domain (path may read domain / Domain name depending on UI skin).
  1. Add a domain name and name-server IP (example: 2.2.2.2 — documentation-style resolver).

docs.title: Step 4: Review and commit
docs.text:
1. Open Commit Manager and review the diff — you should see the new device group and template objects.
  1. Commit.

docs.title: Step 5: Apply the template to the group
docs.text:
1. In Configuration Editor → ncs:devices, open the device group ios-xr-devices.
  2. Click Actions.
  1. Click Apply-Template.
  1. Select your template name (scroll if needed).
  1. Run Apply-Template.

The action result should show ok for each member device.

Templates vs services

Templates are flexible but lack service lifecycle semantics. Uncontrolled edits on devices can drift from the template intent — services (Lab 8) add structure and reconciliation.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
On xr-1, confirm DNS settings from the template:

ssh admin@198.51.100.2

show run domain

Expected output:

domain name-server 2.2.2.2

(Additional domain name lines may appear if you set them in the template.)

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Apply-Template returns failed or only one device updates.

Cause Authgroup mapping, device lock, or template path not committed.

Fix Verify both devices in the group share credentials; check Commit Manager for errors, then sync-from on failing device.

Symptom: show run domain is empty after a successful apply.

Cause Template targeted wrong NED branch or DNS not under domain in your IOS-XR model.

Fix Re-open the template in Configuration Editor, confirm **Domain** leaves exist in the diff, commit, and re-run Apply-Template.

If apply-template fails for all devices or leaves partial state, roll back the last commits or follow Reset the Lab.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Generate a python-and-template service package with ncs-make-package.
  • Define YANG inputs, map them into an IOS-XR XML template, compile, and reload packages.
  • Deploy a service, intentionally remove configuration on devices to create drift, then re-deploy to restore intent.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Service skeletonImplement & testVerify CLI
  • Service skeleton — 7 min
  • Implement & test — 19 min
  • Verify CLI — 4 min

Total — 30 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Lab 7: Device Groups & Templates completed — you are comfortable with Commit Manager and device configuration.
  • NSO instance path matches your environment (examples use ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/).

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text: Unlike templates, services attach lifecycle semantics: NSO tracks what a service applied and can detect drift when someone changes the network outside the service.

docs.title: Context: services vs templates
docs.text: Feature Template Service Push config to multiple devices Yes Yes Detect if config was changed No Yes (check-sync) Re-deploy to restore config No Yes Rollback service-level changes No Yes

docs.title: Step 1: Inspect ncs-make-package
docs.text:
ncs-make-package -h 2>&1 | head -n 20

Expected output:

… python-and-template …

(Use ncs-make-package -h without piping if your shell lacks head.)

docs.title: Step 2: Create the service skeleton
docs.text:
Run the generator inside the instance packages/ directory so you do not need to move folders afterward.

Run inside packages/

If you generate elsewhere, copy the resulting folder into ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/packages/ before compiling.

cd ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/packages/
ncs-make-package --service-skeleton python-and-template STATIC

docs.title: Step 3: Understand the package layout
docs.text:
Open the project in VS Code and expand the explorer to the packages folder.

After the script finishes, STATIC/ should resemble:

STATIC/
├── package-meta-data.xml
├── src/
│   └── yang/
│       └── STATIC.yang
├── templates/
│   └── STATIC-template.xml
└── python/
    └── STATIC/
        └── main.py

package-meta-data.xml contains the service version and other metadata.

docs.title: Step 4: Capture XML with commit dry-run
docs.text:
Build the target IOS-XR static route in the NSO CLI, then export XML with commit dry-run outformat xml — do not leave the candidate committed.

ncs_cli -Cu admin

Example session (prompts may differ):

admin@ncs# config
admin@ncs(config)# devices device xr-1 config
admin@ncs(config-config)# router static
admin@ncs(config-static)# address-family ipv4 unicast
admin@ncs(config-static-afi)# 11.11.11.11/32 10.1.1.2 metric 100 description "set by NSO"
admin@ncs(config-static-afi)# commit dry-run outformat xml

You should see XML shaped like the fragment below (namespaces may vary by NED build):

<devices xmlns="http://tail-f.com/ns/ncs">
  <device>
    <name>xr-1</name>
    <config>
      <router xmlns="http://tail-f.com/ned/cisco-ios-xr">
        <static>
          <address-family>
            <ipv4>
              <unicast>
                <routes-ip>
                  <net>11.11.11.11/32</net>
                  <address>10.1.1.2</address>
                  <metric2>
                    <metric>100</metric>
                  </metric2>
                  <description>set by NSO</description>
                </routes-ip>
              </unicast>
            </ipv4>
          </address-family>
        </static>
      </router>
    </config>
  </device>
</devices>

Do not commit the dry-run candidate

Exit without committing the exploratory static route — answer no if asked to commit, or end / exit per your CLI help.

Uncommitted changes found, commit them? [yes/no/CANCEL] no

docs.title: Step 5: Populate the service template
docs.text:
The generated skeleton includes an XML template and a YANG model — you will edit both.

Copy only the router subtree (inside <config>) into STATIC-template.xml:

<router xmlns="http://tail-f.com/ned/cisco-ios-xr">
  <static>
    <address-family>
      <ipv4>
        <unicast>
          <routes-ip>
            <net>11.11.11.11/32</net>
            <address>10.1.1.2</address>
            <metric2>
              <metric>100</metric>
            </metric2>
            <description>set by NSO</description>
          </routes-ip>
        </unicast>
      </ipv4>
    </address-family>
  </static>
</router>

docs.title: Step 6: Add YANG variables
docs.text:
Replace literals with service inputs. Typical leaves:

  1. Destination prefix (for example 11.11.11.11/32)
  2. Forwarding address (for example 10.1.1.2)
  3. Metric (default 100)
  4. Description (default set by NSO)

docs.title: Update STATIC-template.xml
docs.text: Reference those leaves with your template engine’s variable syntax (for example {$DEST} / {$CONTEXT} patterns — follow the generated skeleton comments).

docs.title: Update STATIC.yang
docs.text: Make dest-prefix and fwd-address mandatory; give metric and description defaults (exact syntax follows your YANG tutorial).

docs.title: Step 7: Compile the package
docs.text:
cd ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/packages/STATIC/src
make

Expected output:

… ncsc … -o ../load-dir/STATIC.fxs yang/STATIC.yang

(Warnings policy depends on your flags; --fail-on-warnings may stop the build if YANG has issues.)

docs.title: Step 8: Reload packages
docs.text:
1. Web UI → ncs:packages → Actions → Reload.
  2. Confirm result is true.
  1. Verify package STATIC appears.

docs.title: Step 9: Deploy the service instance
docs.text:
1. Homepage → Services.
  1. Select the STATIC service.
  1. + Add service — click the + button.
  1. Name it (for example static-11.11.11.11) and fill dest-prefix, fwd-address.

Service created — now attach devices.

  1. Attach devices xr-1 and xr-2 (defaults for metric/description if shown).

  1. Commit Manager → config — inspect diff; use native config if offered.

  1. Commit.

docs.title: Step 10: Test lifecycle (intentional drift)
docs.text:
1. Service Manager — service should be green / in-sync after commit.
  1. Simulate a problem (required for this lab): on each device, Device Manager → Edit Config, delete the static route the service applied.
  1. Commit the deletion on the device context.
  1. Return to Service Manager → Check-Sync — expect a red / out-of-sync indication.
  1. Run Re-deploy on the service — NSO should push intent back to devices until the service is green again.

Key takeaway

Services are self-healing for configuration they own: drift is visible, and re-deploy reapplies the service’s intended configuration.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
On xr-1, confirm the static route exists after re-deploy (documentation address — use yours):

ssh admin@198.51.100.2

show run router static

Expected output:

router static
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  11.11.11.11/32 10.1.1.2 description "set by NSO" metric 100

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: make fails or STATIC.fxs is missing after compile.

Cause YANG error, wrong NED namespace in template, or wrong working directory.

Fix Run `make clean` if present, fix YANG/XML per compiler errors, rebuild from `STATIC/src`.

Symptom: Service stays red after re-deploy or devices reject the route.

Cause Overlapping static routes, VRF mismatch, or device not in service device list.

Fix Verify service instance targets **xr-1** / **xr-2**, check device trace logs, roll back last service commit if needed.

Rollback

To remove this lab’s STATIC package and instance (destructive — coordinate with your class):

ncs --stop 2>/dev/null || true
rm -rf ~/NSO-INSTALL/nso-instance/packages/STATIC
ncs

Then Reload packages in the Web UI and confirm STATIC is gone. Prefer Reset the Lab if the NSO instance is inconsistent.

If re-deploy cannot clear drift or the service model is inconsistent, use Commit Manager rollbacks first, then Reset the Lab if you need a clean VM.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lab you will be able to:

  • Create a local NSO user and attach it to device credentials through an authgroup.
  • Configure NACM groups, rule-lists, and rules that target paths with XPath.
  • Log in as a restricted user and confirm allowed vs denied actions in the Web UI.

docs.title: Time Budget
docs.text:
Users & authgroupsNACM rulesBroaden rule
  • Users & authgroups — 10 min
  • NACM rules — 15 min
  • Broaden rule — 5 min

Total — 30 min

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• You can log in to the NSO Web UI as admin and open Configuration Editor for aaa, nacm, and devices.
  • Device definitions for xr-1 / xr-2 use management addresses consistent with your lab (examples use 198.51.100.2 for xr-1 in XPath samples).

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text: NSO combines AAA users, authgroups (device credential mapping), and NACM (northbound access rules) for fine-grained control.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a read-only test user
docs.text:
1. Open Configuration Editor → aaa:aaa.
  1. Navigate to authentication → users, click Edit config, then + to add a user.
  1. Create read_user (password per your lab policy).
  1. Commit.

You can log out and confirm login as read_user (limited until NACM is configured).

docs.title: Step 2: Map the user through an authgroup
docs.text:
As read_user, open Device Manager — note that ping fails because the user has no authgroup mapping yet.

Log back in as admin to fix this:

  1. Open Configuration Editor → ncs:devices → authgroup (select the group your devices use, often XR).

  1. Inside the authgroup, locate the umap section (maps NSO users to device credentials).

  1. Add read_user with remote-name / remote-password matching device login (admin / cisco123 in many labs).

  1. Commit.

Log in as read_user and open Device Manager — you should now reach connect, ping, and check-sync.

docs.title: Step 3: Create a NACM group and rule-list
docs.text:
1. Log in as admin.
  2. Open Configuration Editor → nacm:nacm.

The NACM module contains rule-list and groups sections.

  1. Create read_group.

  1. Assign group-id (for example 1) and add user read_user.

  1. Under rule-lists, create read_rule.

  1. Attach read_group so all rules in the list apply to that membership.

docs.title: Step 4: Discover XPath targets
docs.text:
Use the NSO CLI to print XPath forms of configuration (paths vary slightly by release):

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show devices device | display xpath" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

Expected output:

/devices/device[name='xr-1']/…
/devices/device[name='xr-2']/…

(Truncated — you need the /devices/device[...] prefixes for rules.)

For deeper paths:

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show configuration devices device xr-1 | display xpath" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

Expected output (illustrative — addresses follow your lab):

/devices/device[name='xr-1']/address 198.51.100.2
/devices/device[name='xr-1']/authgroup XR
/devices/device[name='xr-1']/device-type/cli/ned-id cisco-iosxr-cli-7.x

Access operations

Use the UI help on access-operations to see create, update, delete, exec, and related keywords for your release.

docs.title: Step 5: Create a deny rule for domain configuration on xr-1
docs.text:
Inside the rule-list you have two options: cmdrule (command authorization) and rule (data/rpc authorization).

  1. Inside read_rule, add a rule to deny sync-from or domain access.

  1. In the rule data-node, set the path using the XPath from Step 4: /devices/device[name='xr-1']/config/cisco-ios-xr:domain
  2. Action: deny
  3. Access-operations: create, update, delete, exec (adjust to your NACM schema).

Use the info button on access-operations to see all available keywords.

  1. Commit.

docs.title: Step 6: Test as read_user
docs.text:
1. Log out, then log in as read_user.
  2. Open Device Manager.

You should observe:

  • sync-from may be hidden or disabled (depending on remaining default rules).

  • On xr-1, domain configuration is not visible or not editable.

  • On xr-2, domain may still appear if the rule only names xr-1.

docs.title: Step 7: Broaden the rule to all devices
docs.text:
1. Log in as admin.
  2. Change the rule path from: 
    /devices/device[name='xr-1']/config/cisco-ios-xr:domain
    to: 
    /devices/device/config/cisco-ios-xr:domain
  1. Commit.

Log in as read_user again — domain should be hidden on all managed devices covered by the rule.

Key takeaway

NACM rules target XPath expressions — from entire subtrees down to single leaves — to control who can see or change configuration.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Confirm read_user still exists (admin session):

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show configuration aaa authentication users user read_user" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

Expected output:

user read_user {

(Additional password / key lines may follow — you only need to see the read_user stanza.)

Confirm NACM rules in the Web UI under Configuration Editor → nacm:nacm — read_rule should list deny-config-domain. Optional CLI (output varies):

source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc
echo "show configuration nacm nacm rule-list read_rule" | ncs_cli -u admin -C

docs.title: Common Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: read_user sees the same UI as admin or changes still apply after…

Cause Session cache, wrong user, or rule path typo / wrong NACM group attachment.

Fix Log out fully, clear site data if needed, re-check **groups** ↔ **read_user** mapping and XPath spelling (including namespace prefix).

Symptom: ncs_cli XPath commands return errors or empty.

Cause Wrong `-u` user, environment not sourced, or display xpath not supported in your build.

Fix Run `source ~/NSO-INSTALL/ncsrc` first; use **Configuration Editor** copy-path features as a fallback.

If you lock yourself out or rules conflict, log in as admin, revert the last NACM commits in Commit Manager, or use Reset the Lab for snapshot restore.

docs.title: Accessibility spot-check log (WCAG 2.1 Level AA baseline)
docs.text:
This log records manual and tool-assisted checks for the learner site (NFR-A1 / Story 3.7). Formal release gating may add automated axe / Lighthouse in Story 3.11.

Date Auditor Scope (pages) Tool / method AA violations Notes 2026-04-17 Project index.md, Lab 1 (01-connect-workstation.md), Lab 8 (08-create-service.md) Manual + keyboard tab order 0 open Baseline after Story 3.7: skip links (content, navigation, verification on lab pages), Cisco focus rings on interactive controls, global aria-live announcer for copy; paired blocks retain polite live regions.

Follow-ups (if any): none at baseline.

docs.title: Cisco NSO Workbook — Authoring Guide
docs.text: This guide is the single canonical reference for voice, structure, and lint for lab chapters. Use it when writing or reviewing Markdown under docs/.

docs.title: Prerequisites & local build
docs.text: Target (NFR-M3): From git clone to make serve with a working site in ≤ 30 minutes on a standard Cisco corporate laptop.

docs.title: Tooling
docs.text:
Tool Version Notes Python 3.12 See .python-version. Use pyenv or Cisco-blessed Python. GNU Make Any macOS/Linux; Windows use WSL2 (native PowerShell not supported). Browser Modern Chrome, Firefox, or Edge. git Any For clone and commits.

macOS

brew install pyenv
pyenv install 3.12
pyenv local 3.12
python --version
make --version

Linux (Ubuntu / Debian)

sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y make python3.12 python3.12-venv

Windows: use WSL2 and follow Linux steps inside the Ubuntu terminal.

docs.title: Clone and install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/daquezad/NSO-hands-on-session.git <YOUR_DIR>
cd <YOUR_DIR>
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

<YOUR_DIR> is the only placeholder — replace it with your local path.

docs.title: Build and serve
docs.text:
Command Purpose make serve Live reload at http://localhost:8000 (learner build). make build-learner Static site → site/. make build-instructor Instructor site → site-instructor/ (INSTRUCTOR=1). make lint Authoring lint (AR13 rules 1–11 + AR15). Default warn mode (exit 0). LINT_RULES_1_3_MODE=fail / LINT_RULES_4_7_MODE=fail / LINT_RULES_5_6_MODE=fail / LINT_RULES_8_11_MODE=fail hard-fail matching rule groups (rule 8 stays warning-only). make pdf-learner / make pdf-instructor PDF via Chromium headless (see Makefile). make rollback-lab LAB=N Story 3.9 — prints non-destructive rollback hints for chapter N (1–9); see docs/reset-lab.md for full VM / NSO procedures. make ci-quality-gates Story 3.11 — npm ci then scripts/ci/run_quality_gates.sh (perf budget, links, Lighthouse, axe); requires Chromium or Chrome on PATH.

Learner journey (Story 3.8): mkdocs.yml defines the left navigation (all chapters + Reset the Lab). Material’s table of contents follows the current page (toc.follow in mkdocs.yml). Search is the built-in offline index (plugins: search) — no network at runtime. After make build-learner, run python3 scripts/check_noindex.py site/ to confirm every generated .html includes a robots meta with noindex and nofollow and that site/sitemap.xml is absent (CI runs this step on every PR).

Responsive / touch (Story 3.10): At ≤768px width, Material collapses primary nav to the hamburger; a Contents pill (in overrides/main.html) toggles the same #__toc control as the in-page table of contents. ≥44×44px touch targets are enforced for header actions and code-copy buttons in extra.css. The time_budget legend stacks vertically at ≤600px; journey_table uses stacked cards at ≤600px (see extra.css). Record spot-checks in docs/responsive-check-log.md.

CI quality gates (Story 3.11 / 6.10): PR workflow runs scripts/check_perf_budget.py (≤500 KB HTML+CSS+JS per page, NFR-P5), scripts/check_internal_links.py (NFR-R4), scripts/check_noindex.py (NFR-S8), Lighthouse performance ≥90 on home + Lab 8 (mobile + simulated throttling, NFR-P7), and scripts/check_axe_warn.py (axe-core against URLs in scripts/ci/axe-pages.yaml; default AXE_MODE=warn with optional a11y_baseline.yaml ceilings; set AXE_MODE=fail in CI to block on serious/critical — NFR-A8 / FR39). Locally: make ci-quality-gates (needs npm ci and Chromium/Chrome on PATH). See docs/_internal/accessibility.md.

Lint cross-reference: Rules 1–3 — frontmatter, filename, headings (Story 2.3). Rule 3d — every docs/NN-*.md chapter must call time_budget(...) (Story 3.4). Rule 3f — docs/index.md home cover macros (Story 3.6). Rules 4a–4d, 7 — language tags, Expected output pairing, bare commands, expected_output landmark (Story 3.3), Jinja allow-list includes topology, home_subtitle, home_meta, journey_table. Rules 5–6 — alt text, links (Story 2.5). Rules 8–10 — instructor coherence, versions, classification (Story 2.6). Rule 11 — lab-safety partial + LAB_SAFETY_MESSAGE + lab_safety only on index (general) and Lab 8 (intentional_failure) (Story 3.4). Rule 12 — instructor block parity (Story 5.7).

docs.title: What you should see
docs.text: After make serve, the home page shows the workbook title, navigation for Labs 1–9, and Material chrome (search, dark mode). If the page is blank, confirm the venv is active and you are in the repo root (directory containing mkdocs.yml).

docs.title: Timing log (NFR-M3 feedback loop)
docs.text: If onboarding takes longer than 30 minutes, file a GitHub issue titled onboarding: exceeded 30-minute SLA — <date> — <OS> and add a row to docs/onboarding-log.md with your timings.

docs.title: Anatomy of a lab chapter
docs.text:
Copy docs/_template/chapter-template.md to docs/NN-your-lab-title.md where NN is a two-digit chapter index (01–99). Fill YAML frontmatter and all six mandatory sections.

Mandatory section order (AR14):

  1. ## Learning Objectives
  2. ## Time Budget
  3. ## Prerequisites
  4. ## Procedure
  5. ## Verification
  6. ## Common Errors

Machine-readable schema: docs/_template/schema.yaml. Lint rule 1 validates frontmatter against that schema; lint rule 3 enforces section order, a single H1, and no skipped heading levels (e.g. H1 → H3 without H2).

Story 3.4 — section macros

Macro Where time_budget(total=N, segments=[[minutes,"label"],...]) Under ## Time Budget on every docs/NN-*.md lab chapter. Minutes must sum to N. Invoke as a Jinja macro call in Markdown (double curly braces around the call). common_errors_start … common_error(symptom, cause, fix) … common_errors_end Optional, inside ## Common Errors. <!-- lab_safety: LAB_SAFETY_MESSAGE empty in mkdocs.yml --> docs/index.md only. <!-- lab_safety: LAB_SAFETY_MESSAGE empty in mkdocs.yml --> 08-*.md (Lab 8) only.

Do not call lab_safety on labs 01–07 or 09. Lint rules 3d and 11 enforce this.

Story 3.5 — topology + images

Piece Purpose docs/assets/mermaid-sources/<chapter>/<id>.mmd Mermaid source (≤ 8 nodes; names like xr-1, nso-server, …). docs/assets/mermaid-sources/<chapter>/<id>.alt.txt Long description for accessibility; copied next to renders. make pre-render-mermaid Runs scripts/pre_render_mermaid.py → docs/assets/images/mermaid/<chapter>/<id>.{svg,png,alt.txt} (requires Node + @mermaid-js/mermaid-cli; Chromium must launch). Commit generated SVG/PNG/alt. topology(diagram_id, chapter, caption) Embeds the pre-rendered SVG for web (wrap the call in Jinja macro syntax in source). Screenshots Drop PNGs under docs/assets/images/ (see subfolders as needed). make optimize-images runs oxipng when installed. WebP variants for PNGs are produced at mkdocs build time (not committed) when cwebp is on PATH.

Story 3.6 — home cover (docs/index.md)

Macro Role home_subtitle(text="…") One-line audience line directly under the H1. home_meta() Meta row: total time (from journey data), 9 labs, NSO version from mkdocs.yml extra.nso_version. journey_table() Accessible <table> with caption; Lab 8 row includes an intentional break chip with visible text.

Order (required): H1 headline → home_subtitle → home_meta → lab_safety(general) → topology → journey_table. No generic “welcome to…” copy. Lint rule 3f enforces the macros and a single H1.

Story 3.7 — accessibility baseline

Skip links (Material “Skip to content” plus Skip to navigation and Skip to verification on pages that have ## Verification), Cisco-blue focus rings on Material controls, global copy announcer (#css-a11y-announcer), and docs/a11y-log.md for spot-check records.

docs.title: Voice & style (UX-DR27)
docs.text: Reviewer-gated (not automatically linted). AI-generated content must follow this section.

docs.title: Second person; present indicative imperatives
docs.text:
Right

Run `devices sync-from`. Verify the device reaches sync-result `true`.

Wrong

Now you will run `devices sync-from`, and we'll check that sync-result is true.

Lint: — (reviewer / FR42)

docs.title: CLI prompts are literal: user@ncs> — never $ or >
docs.text:
Right

At the `user@ncs>` prompt, run `show devices list`.

Wrong

At `$` run `show devices list`.

Lint: — (reviewer); rule 4c will reject $ / > inside command fences when rules 4–7 are enforced.

docs.title: Cisco capitalization: NSO, NED, CLI, YANG (all caps); command names in backticks every time
docs.text:
Right

Use the NSO CLI to commit the YANG-backed change. Run `commit dry-run` first.

Wrong

Use the Nso cli to commit the yang change.

Lint: — (reviewer)

docs.title: Banned: "As you know…" and assumed prior knowledge
docs.text:
Right

If you have not completed Lab 3, stop and finish it before this step.

Wrong

As you know, NSO stores configuration in CDB.

Lint: — (reviewer)

docs.title: Density rules (UX-DR28)
docs.text:
Limits: ≤ 3 sentences per paragraph; ≤ 5 paired command/output blocks per ## Procedure subsection; blank line between paired blocks and the next step or ## Common Errors.

Right (short paragraph; one paired block; blank line before next step)

Sync devices from NSO. Run devices sync-from. Show result true in the sync summary before you continue.

Wrong (one overloaded paragraph; violates ≤ 3 sentences and hurts scanning)

Sync is important because it pulls config and you should always sync before commit and if sync fails you need to check the trace and also verify NED versions because mismatches cause errors and then commit might not work.

Lint: prose density warnings planned; not in rules 1–3.

docs.title: Callout scarcity (UX-DR29)
docs.text:
• Lab-safety banner: only home and Lab 8 (architecture / UX-DR29).
  • !!! danger: ≤ 1 per lab.
  • !!! warning: ≤ 3 per lab.
  • !!! note / !!! tip: allowed without a hard cap (still subject to density).

Right

!!! warning "Data loss risk"
    This commit cannot be rolled back automatically.

Wrong

!!! danger "Minor note"
    Casual over-use of danger callouts trains learners to ignore them.

Lint: — (reviewer until rule 11 extended).

docs.title: Allowed admonition types (UX-DR16)
docs.text:
Only these Material types are allowed in v1.0 (architecture Pattern 1). Lint rule 11 will enforce the allow-list.

Type Use !!! note Factual clarification !!! info Reference / side detail !!! tip Shortcut or better workflow !!! warning Could cause data loss or break the lab !!! danger Will break the lab or host !!! example Worked example !!! question Reflection question !!! tip "Instructor" inside an instructor if / endif block Instructor-only hint (≤5 lines)

Banned unless added by PR with semantics: quote, abstract, failure, success, bug, etc.

Right

!!! tip "Faster path"
    Use `commit dry-run` before every commit.

Wrong

!!! success "You did it!"
    Great job.

Lint: rule 11 (Story 2.6).

docs.title: Code and expected-output pattern (UX-DR17)
docs.text:
Every command block uses a language-tagged fence (cli, bash, shell, …). Immediately after (ignoring blank lines), provide an italic *Expected output:* label and a text or console fence — or place <!-- lint-skip: no-output --> before the block to opt out.

Right

```cli
show devices list

Expected output:

NAME    ADDRESS
xr-1    192.0.2.1

**Wrong**

```markdown
```cli
show devices list

(No labelled output fence — learners cannot distinguish input from output.) 

**Lint:** **rules 4a–4c**, **rule 7** (Story 2.4).

### Paired rendering + landmark hint (Story 3.3)

The build wraps each **command fence → *Expected output:* → output fence** sequence into a **paired** block (shared border, label bar, copy on the command half only). Optionally, on the line **before** *Expected output:*, add:


`{{ expected_output(landmark="commit complete") }}`


Use a short **landmark** string (≤ 60 characters) learners should look for in the output. **Lint rule 4d** requires a non-empty `landmark="..."` whenever `expected_output` is used.

---

## Idempotency and rollback

Frontmatter `idempotent: true|false` (see `docs/_template/schema.yaml`). If `idempotent: false`, the chapter **must** include a rollback admonition (typically under `## Common Errors` or end of `## Procedure`) with the exact commands to restore prior state — **Story 2.6** lint will enforce pairing.

**Right**

```markdown
idempotent: false

…and later a !!! warning "Rollback" with the rollback / revert sequence for this lab.

Wrong

idempotent: false

…with no rollback instructions anywhere in the chapter.

Lint: extended rule under Story 2.6.

docs.title: Commit conventions
docs.text:
Use Conventional Commits (feat:, fix:, docs:, ci:, …) and scoped subjects where helpful. Full policy and CI wiring land in Story 2.7.

Right: docs: clarify verification steps in Lab 4 Wrong: updates

docs.title: Instructor-only content (Epic 5)
docs.text:
Dual-audience builds use three mechanisms (AR5):

Mechanism Where Learner site/ Inline Jinja instructor gate (if instructor … endif) Inside docs/NN-*.md Block omitted when instructor is false (macros env) Companion docs/NN-*-instructor-notes.md Next to the lab file File omitted from the build (hooks.py on_files) Shared facilitator docs docs/instructor-artifacts/** e.g. proctor-checklist.md, timing-sheet.md (Stories 5.4–5.5; instructor site only) Omitted from learner builds

make build-learner and default mkdocs serve exclude companion paths and instructor-artifacts/. make build-instructor sets INSTRUCTOR=1 and includes them in site-instructor/.

instructor_block macro (UX-DR12, Story 5.3) — use for pacing, FAQs, and Lab 8 choreography inside a lab chapter. Pass facilitator copy as markdown in the body argument (Jinja allows multiline strings). variant is generic (default) or choreography (Lab 8 extended script). When instructor is false, the macro expands to nothing (no empty wrapper, no hidden DOM). Styling lives in docs/stylesheets/components/instructor-block.css; the macro output includes a stable data attribute used by make build-all smoke checks (lab + _test-fixtures/ HTML only).

Right (instructor_block)

Wrong (instructor_block)

Putting facilitator-only guidance in plain chapter prose with no gate or macro.

docs.title: UX-DR30 minimum content per chapter
docs.text:
Lint rule 12 (Story 5.7) enforces these minimums in every lab chapter docs/NN-*.md (warn-first; see docs/lint-cutover-plan.md).

Requirement Where Notes Expected duration Chapter frontmatter (estimated_duration / schema) Already part of the lab contract; not duplicated inside the macro body. Two learner FAQs Inside instructor_block(variant="generic", …) Use a ### FAQs sub-heading with at least two bullet lines (- or *). One thing that breaks Same generic block Use ### What breaks with at least one bullet. Lab 8 only: choreography Second macro: instructor_block(variant="choreography", …) Requires chapter: 8 in frontmatter. Body uses ### Pause points, ### Narrating check-sync, and ### Red-to-green flip, each with facilitator content (bullets recommended).

Example — generic block

Example — Lab 8 generic + choreography

Right (inline if instructor)

{% if instructor %}
!!! tip "Instructor"
    Allow 5 extra minutes if the class hit sync issues in Lab 2.
{% endif %}

Wrong

Facilitators: skip this section in a 90-minute session.

(Learner-visible facilitator instruction with no gate.)

Do not put facilitator-only timing or answers in learner-visible prose without a gate. Details: AR5, Epic 5 stories 5.1–5.3, lint rule 12 (Story 5.7).

docs.title: Quick reference: run checks locally
docs.text:
make build-learner
make build-all          # learner + instructor static sites (Story 5.2)
make serve-instructor   # optional: live reload with facilitator paths (default :8001)
make lint
# Full fail-mode dry run (same as CI strict cutover):
make lint-fail-all

Cutover sequencing and CI behavior: docs/lint-cutover-plan.md.

Unit tests for the linter:

python -m unittest scripts.tests.test_lint_authoring -v

docs.title: Authoring lint — warn → fail cutover plan
docs.text:
This document tracks AR13 rules and the AR15 rollback extension: current enforcement mode, what blocks in CI today, and how to move to full fail mode after Epic 4 brownfield migration.

Related: CONTRIBUTING.md (lint env vars, Conventional Commits), scripts/lint_authoring.py, make lint / make lint-fail-all.

docs.title: CI today (Story 2.7)
docs.text:
Check Behavior make lint Default warn for all rule groups — prints violations, exit 0 so PRs stay mergeable during migration. make lint-fail-all Sets every LINT_RULES_*_MODE=fail (including rule 12) — use locally or in CI when you want a full enforcement dry run (Epic 4 backlog sizing). PR title Conventional Commits (AR19) enforced in .github/workflows/build.yml — invalid titles fail the workflow.

To block merges on lint violations, change the workflow step from make lint to make lint-fail-all after content is green (or per-group by exporting only the modes you want to enforce).

docs.title: Rule inventory and modes
docs.text:
ID Scope Default mode Env var Notes 1–3, 3d, 3f Structure warn LINT_RULES_1_3_MODE Frontmatter schema, filename, headings; 3d — time_budget(...) on chapters; 3f — docs/index.md home cover (Story 3.6) 4a–d, 7 Fences / macros warn LINT_RULES_4_7_MODE Language tags, expected output, bare commands, expected_output landmark (Story 3.3), Jinja macro name references (rule 7) 5–6 Alt / links / URLs warn LINT_RULES_5_6_MODE Allowlist: scripts/url_allowlist.txt 8 Instructor coherence warning only — Never fails exit code; listed under LINT_RULES_8_11_MODE group for messaging 9–11, AR15 Versions, classification, lab safety, rollback warn LINT_RULES_8_11_MODE Rule 11 includes Story 3.4 lab_safety placement (index + Lab 8 only); rule 8 warnings print in the same rollout window 12 UX-DR30 instructor_block (generic FAQs + What breaks; Lab 8 choreography) warn LINT_RULES_12_MODE Story 5.7 — lands warn-first; chapters need instructor_block backfill (Epic 4 residuals or pre–v1.0 content passes)

Rule 8 does not set a fail bit; only rules 9–11 and AR15 in the 8_11 group respect LINT_RULES_8_11_MODE=fail.

Rule 12 respects LINT_RULES_12_MODE=fail independently (not bundled with the 8_11 group).

docs.title: Planned cutover (fill in at release planning)
docs.text:
Milestone Target Actions Epic 4 complete 2026-04-17 (labs 1–9 migrated) Enable lint-fail-all in CI when ready; make lint is clean in warn mode with zero violations. Post-migration TBD Optionally split CI: fail on 1_3 first, then 4_7, etc. Rule 12 (UX-DR30) Story 5.7 (2026-04-17) Shipped warn by default; track instructor-block backfill per lab; promote with Epic 6 release gate when make lint-fail-all is green for rule 12

Update this table when enforcement dates are agreed; optional follow-up is automated drift detection between this doc and lint_authoring.py (nice-to-have).

docs.title: Reset the Lab
docs.text: When exercises leave NSO, devices, or the Linux desktop in an unknown state, use this appendix to return to a known-good baseline before retrying a chapter or the full workbook.

docs.title: Before you reset
docs.text:
1. Save work you still need (notes, exports) outside the lab VM if your environment allows it.
  2. Prefer NSO-native undo when the problem is limited to recent commits: open Commit Manager, review rollback files, and load the one that matches the state you want (see 5. Rollbacks).
  3. Snapshot restore is the fastest recovery when multiple subsystems are corrupted or you are unsure what changed.

docs.title: VM-level reset (clean slate)
docs.text:
Your hosted lab environment typically provides snapshot or reprovision controls (name varies by platform). Use the vendor flow to restore the initial snapshot for this workbook.

After the VM is fresh:

  1. Confirm you can open a terminal on linux-host (Lab 1).
  2. Walk forward through labs in order, or jump to your chapter only if you already know earlier labs are clean.

Example check after reconnecting:

hostname

Expected output:

linux-host

(Illustrative — your VM may show a different stable hostname.)

docs.title: NSO rollback (configuration undo)
docs.text:
For mistakes confined to recent NSO commits, use the Web UI:

  1. Commit Manager → Load/Save
  2. Select the rollback file dated before the bad change
  3. Load → review the staged candidate → Commit

This pattern is the primary undo path for Labs 4–9 when CDB changes are the issue.

# Optional — from a shell on linux-host, confirm NSO is running (paths may match your install tree)
pgrep -a java | head -3

Expected output:

# one or more Java processes (NSO JVM) — exact lines depend on your release

docs.title: Per-chapter expectations (idempotency)
docs.text:
During Epic 4 migration, each docs/NN-*.md chapter will declare idempotent: true|false in YAML frontmatter. Until that metadata is present for every chapter, use this working guidance:

Lab Chapter Safe to repeat? If stuck 1 Connect to the Workstation Usually yes Fix console / desktop; snapshot if unusable 2 Install NSO and NEDs Re-run cautiously Reload packages; snapshot if install is corrupt 3 Register XRd Routers Often yes Remove bad devices or restore snapshot 4 Configure Devices Yes with rollbacks Commit Manager rollback files 5 Rollbacks Yes Use this chapter as the rollback reference 6 Out-of-Band Sync Yes Check-Sync + Sync-To / Sync-From 7 Device Groups & Templates Yes Re-apply or rollback commits 8 Create a Service Yes (expect drift drills) Re-deploy service; roll back commits as needed 9 RBAC Access Control Yes Revert NACM rules via Commit Manager

If a chapter includes a !!! warning "Rollback" admonition (required when idempotent: false in frontmatter), follow those commands first; use this appendix if you still cannot converge.

docs.title: Makefile helper
docs.text:
From the repo root (developer workstation — not required on the lab VM), contributors can run:

make rollback-lab LAB=5

That invokes scripts/rollback-lab.sh, which prints non-destructive reminders keyed to chapter 1–9. It does not SSH into your environment or run ncs commands for you.

docs.title: Screenshot and diagram scrub protocol
docs.text: Follow this protocol before you merge any image that could show customer networks, identities, or operational data. Use second-person, imperative steps (UX-DR27).

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This protocol applies to:

  1. Screenshots — PNG/JPEG/WebP captures of terminals, browsers, NSO Web UI, device CLIs, or OS windows.
  2. Diagrams that embed text — Exported images (PNG/SVG) where hostnames, IPs, or labels are baked into pixels or paths.
  3. PDF images — Any raster copied into the workbook PDF pipeline from the same sources as (1) and (2).

It does not replace alt text or Mermaid source hygiene: you still satisfy authoring lint rule 5 and pre-rendered diagram alt files as described in docs/authoring.md.

docs.title: Identifiers to remove
docs.text:
For each category below, scrub before commit. Pair each category with a practical check and a safe replacement.

Identifier Detection heuristic Replace with Customer hostnames FQDNs not in the approved list (see next section); host labels that match a customer naming convention visible in the shot Use names from the approved list (xr-1, nso-server, linux-host, …) or generic placeholders (router-a, nms-host) consistent with UX-DR11 topology naming. Real public IPs IPv4/IPv6 that are not in RFC 5737 documentation ranges; addresses that match your organization’s live allocation RFC 5737 documentation ranges only: 192.0.2.0/24, 198.51.100.0/24, 203.0.113.0/64 (IPv4 examples); for IPv6 use 2001:db8::/32 per RFC 3849. MAC addresses Six octet patterns (xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx, vendor OUIs that identify customer gear) Fixed lab values such as 00:1a:2b:3c:4d:5e (document that they are synthetic in the chapter if needed). Serial numbers / PIDs Long alphanumeric strings on chassis labels, show inventory, license dialogs Redact to SN-REDACTED or a short synthetic token (CHASSIS-001). IGP / LDAP user identifiers Real usernames in prompts, whoami, directory UIs Replace with lab accounts (admin, cisco, learner) already used in the workbook narrative. Customer organization names Logos, banners, email domains, “Company X” in titles Use Example Org or Lab Org; remove logos. License key fragments Long hex/base64 strings in entitlement or install dialogs Crop or blur the region; do not transcribe. Clearly internal Cisco project codenames Non-public program names visible in slides or tickets Replace with a neutral label (Internal project) or remove the line.

docs.title: Identifiers permitted
docs.text:
Use these consistently so learners and screenshots stay aligned with the lab topology (UX-DR11):

  • Device names: xr-1, xr-2, nso-server, linux-host (and names listed in your chapter’s topology macro).
  • Documentation IPv4: Any address inside 192.0.2.0/24, 198.51.100.0/24, or 203.0.113.0/24 (RFC 5737).
  • Documentation IPv6: 2001:db8::/32 (RFC 3849) unless your lab file specifies another documented range.
  • Lab accounts explicitly taught in the workbook (e.g. admin / documented passwords in fenced blocks only when required by the lab).

If you are unsure whether a string is permitted, scrub it.

docs.title: Scrub tools
docs.text:
1. In-image editing — Crop sensitive regions; use solid fills or blur for small areas; redraw simple UI chrome if needed.
  2. OS-level redaction — macOS Preview, Snipping Tool + editor, or GIMP for redact boxes before export.
  3. Re-capture — Prefer a clean capture from a sanitized lab VM over heavy editing when time allows.

Re-export to PNG (or WebP for web-only) and keep file sizes within NFR-P6 budgets in docs/authoring.md.

docs.title: Worked example
docs.text:
Scenario: A terminal screenshot showed a customer hostname and a routable public IP in a ping line.

Asset Role docs/assets/images/scrub-example/before.png Stand-in “before” image (synthetic 1×1 pixel — replace with a real before/only example in your branch when illustrating the guide). docs/assets/images/scrub-example/after.png Stand-in “after” scrub (documentation-safe palette).

Diff narrative: Remove the customer FQDN and public IP from the visible line; replace with xr-1 and an address from 198.51.100.0/24; re-run ping in the lab VM if needed so the capture matches the story. Crop scrollback if unrelated sessions leak identifiers.

docs.title: Author self-review
docs.text:
Before you open a PR that adds or changes images in chapter NN:

  1. List every raster under docs/assets/images/ referenced from docs/NN-*.md (and any Mermaid PNG/SVG used by that chapter).
  2. Walk the Identifiers to remove table above for each file.
  3. Confirm alt text is present and describes the scrubbed content (lint rule 5).
  4. Add or update a dated row in docs/scrub-logs/NN.md with your name, date, and “pass — protocol 4.1”.

docs.title: Second-pair review (NFR-S6)
docs.text:
At v0.9 (pre-release cohort readiness):

  1. A second CX engineer (not the chapter author) selects 10% of chapters at random, rounding up — for 9 labs, review at least 1 chapter (if 10 chapters, review 2).
  2. For each selected chapter, open every image referenced from that chapter’s Markdown and verify compliance with Identifiers to remove and Identifiers permitted.
  3. Record a dated sign-off in docs/scrub-logs/second-pair-review.md: reviewer name, chapter(s), date, pass/fail, and short notes on any fix-ups required.

Random selection can be a simple spreadsheet draw or shuf -n 1 on the list 01–09 — document the method in the log entry.
